claims.text: Technology capable of producing unlimited clean free energy has existed for over 100 years.

evidence.quote:
the technology to
create unlimited clean free energy has
existed for over a 100 years

claims.rationale: No peer-reviewed controlled experiment has ever demonstrated net-positive energy output from any of the devices cited. The first and second laws of thermodynamics are among the most empirically tested principles in physics; a 100-year conspiracy would require sustained coordination across multiple sovereign patent offices, oil majors, and the physics community.

claims.text: This episode will not take a side in the climate-change debate.

evidence.quote:
the point of this
episode isn't to argue one side of the
climate debate or the other

claims.rationale: This is a framing statement by the host defining the scope of the episode; it is a performative definitional declaration rather than an empirical claim, and can be evaluated by observing the episode itself.

claims.text: Global warming is a myth.

evidence.quote:
global warming is a myth it's
not a
myth

claims.rationale: The human-presented character advances this line; the host (AJ) immediately rejects it with 'it's not a myth.' Anthropogenic warming is supported by multiple independent lines of evidence (instrumental record, isotopic signatures, basic radiative physics) and is the consensus of every major scientific academy.

claims.text: In 1936 Charles Pogue was issued a patent for a high-mileage carburetor.

evidence.quote:
in 1936 POG was issued a
patent for a high mileage carburetor

claims.rationale: Charles N. Pogue's carburetor patents (US 1,938,497 and follow-ups) are in the US patent record from the early-to-mid 1930s; the existence of the patent itself is easy to verify.

claims.text: The Pogue carburetor delivered roughly 200 miles per gallon in independent tests.

evidence.quote:
that's almost 220 MP gallon
Ford tested it they got 200 m per gallon

claims.rationale: Gasoline's thermochemical energy density caps theoretical best-case mileage for a ~3500 lb V8 of that era below ~80 mpg even with perfect combustion and zero aerodynamic drag; claims of 200+ mpg have never been reproduced under controlled instrumented conditions. Original test reports were promotional, not blinded.

claims.text: After the Pogue carburetor was publicized, oil-company stocks crashed on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

evidence.quote:
on the Toronto Stock Exchange Oil
Company stock prices crashed

claims.rationale: No contemporary TSX price record or financial-press report from 1936 documents an oil-stock crash coinciding with Pogue's carburetor publicity. This detail circulates exclusively in conspiracy secondary literature and should be treated as unverified folklore.

claims.text: In 1951 the United States passed the Invention Secrecy Act.

evidence.quote:
in 1951 the United States passed
The Invention secrecy act

claims.rationale: The Invention Secrecy Act of 1951 (Pub.L. 82-256, codified at 35 U.S.C. 181-188) is a verifiable US federal law still in force. As of recent FAS reporting, several thousand secrecy orders are active at any time.

claims.text: Tom Ogle's modified 1970 Ford Galaxy achieved 100 miles per gallon.

evidence.quote:
his 1970 Ford Galaxy got 11 m
per gallon but after a few modifications
it got 100 m per gallon

claims.rationale: Ogle's April 1977 El Paso demonstration was an open-road drive, not a dyno-instrumented test with independent fuel measurement. No subsequent engineering team reproduced the figure. A 4000-lb V8 achieving 100 mpg on a pure-hydrocarbon fuel cycle is far outside the thermodynamic ceiling for the engine design.

claims.text: Shell Oil offered Tom Ogle $25 million cash for his carburetor design.

evidence.quote:
Shell Oil offered him $25 million cash
for the design Ogle passed

claims.rationale: This detail comes from Ogle and his attorney's retelling and has been repeated in fringe literature; Shell Oil has no record of the offer, and no corroborating business filings or press reports from 1977-78 substantiate a $25M corporate acquisition overture of this scale.

claims.text: Tom Ogle was shot by a stranger outside a bar in 1978.

evidence.quote:
Ogle was shot by
a stranger outside a bar

claims.rationale: El Paso police records and contemporaneous local press did document a shooting incident involving Ogle in April 1978; he survived. His death four months later was ruled an overdose. Whether the shooting was random or targeted was never determined.

claims.text: Stanley Meyer invented a car that runs on nothing but water.

evidence.quote:
he had invented a car that can run
on nothing but water

claims.rationale: Electrolyzing water into H2 and O2 and then burning them recovers at most the energy used for electrolysis minus losses - net energy is negative. A 1996 Ohio court (Fayette County) found Meyer guilty of 'gross and egregious fraud' against two investors who bought into the water-fuel-cell promise, and no independent engineering test has verified the claim.

claims.text: Stanley Meyer was poisoned.

evidence.quote:
Stan's final words were
they poisoned
me

claims.rationale: Meyer's death was investigated by the Grove City, Ohio police; the official ruling was cerebral aneurysm, and toxicology screens did not find poisons. Meyer's statement 'they poisoned me' at the moment of collapse is first-person testimony under acute medical distress - it is primary evidence but not corroborated.

claims.text: Stanley Meyer's water engine violates the second law of thermodynamics.

evidence.quote:
they say Stan technology
broke the second law of Thermodynamics

claims.rationale: The skeptic's characterization cited by the host is correct. A device that extracts more combustion energy from water than the electrical energy used to split it is a perpetual-motion machine of the second kind. The host then dismisses this with a rhetorical 'maybe the laws are wrong' move that is not supported by any peer-reviewed result.

dependencies.rationale: [logical] If Meyer's engine violates 2LoT, the claim that it works as described cannot be simultaneously true.

claims.text: Enough zero-point energy exists in a coffee cup of empty space to boil the world's oceans.

evidence.quote:
there's enough
energy in the volume of a coffee cup to
evaporate all the world's oceans

claims.rationale: The figure originates from a naive reading of a QED vacuum-energy density upper bound at the Planck scale; the extractable zero-point energy in any reference frame is operationally zero, because energy is defined relative to the vacuum itself. No apparatus has ever drawn net power from vacuum fluctuations.

claims.text: Floyd Sweet's VTA device produced 224,000 watts from 0.3 milliwatts of input.

evidence.quote:
he fed it 0.3 M
and produced almost 224,000 Watts

claims.rationale: A gain of ~7.5x10^8 in power without an identified energy reservoir violates energy conservation. Sweet's demonstrations were filmed for promotional videos by Tom Bearden; no controlled third-party lab ever instrumented input and output under replicable conditions. The measurement artifacts (e.g. inductive pickup, battery hidden in device housing) are standard explanations that were never ruled out.

claims.text: Within 24 hours of Sweet's death the FBI confiscated all his equipment and research.

evidence.quote:
the
FBI confiscated all Sparky's equipment
and research

claims.rationale: This seizure is reported only in Bearden's secondary narrative; no FOIA-released FBI case file, property-receipt record, or contemporary news item documents it. The FBI does not routinely seize inventor estates without a criminal-case predicate.

claims.text: In 2010 the Federation of American Scientists counted 5,135 inventions placed under secrecy orders in that single year.

evidence.quote:
in that year alone
5,135 inventions were given a secrecy
order

claims.rationale: FAS's Secrecy News tracker does publish annual totals of active secrecy orders; the ~5,000 figure is consistent with the active-orders total for 2010 (cumulative, not orders imposed that year specifically). The host conflates 'active' with 'new' - a common reporting error - but the underlying tally itself is real and public.

claims.text: In 1971 a 20% efficient solar panel was patented and made secret.

evidence.quote:
in 1971 over 50 years ago
someone patented a solar panel that was
20% efficient it was made secret

claims.rationale: Lab-scale solar cells did reach ~20% efficiency in the early 1970s (notably at Bell Labs and COMSAT), but reaching that efficiency in mass-manufactured panels required decades of materials-science iteration. The specific 1971 'secrecy-ordered' 20% solar patent cited is not identifiable in Patent Office records released to date.

claims.text: Commercial solar panels last only 15 to 20 years before decaying into toxic landfill waste.

evidence.quote:
solar panels only last about 15 to 20
years

claims.rationale: Industry data (NREL, IEA PVPS) typically quote crystalline-silicon panel lifetimes of 25-30 years with ~0.5% annual degradation; the 15-20 figure understates real-world lifespans. Crystalline Si panels are not classified as hazardous waste in most jurisdictions; the 'toxic' framing applies mainly to some thin-film chemistries (e.g. CdTe) and broken-glass leachate, not to the dominant c-Si technology.

claims.text: Modern green energy is a scam.

evidence.quote:
green
energy it's a
scam

claims.rationale: Lifecycle analyses published across independent research groups (MIT, IEA, IPCC working groups) consistently find that solar, wind, and nuclear all produce significantly lower lifecycle CO2 per kWh than fossil generation, even accounting for manufacturing and end-of-life. The 'scam' framing is polemical; the underlying critiques of cost overruns, subsidy capture, and supply-chain ethics have kernels of truth but do not support the sweeping verdict.

claims.text: Over 80% of US electricity comes from coal, oil, and natural gas.

evidence.quote:
over 80% of electricity
comes from coal oil and natural gas

claims.rationale: US EIA data for 2023-2024 show fossil-fuel-sourced electricity at roughly 60%, with the balance from nuclear (~19%) and renewables (~21%). The 80% figure was accurate in the early 2000s but overstates the current mix by about 20 percentage points.

claims.text: Salvatore Pais holds a patent for a deck-of-cards-sized device that generates unlimited clean energy from the vacuum.

evidence.quote:
a patent for a tiny
solid state device about the size of a
deck of cards that can generate
unlimited clean energy from the vacuum

claims.rationale: Pais's US Navy-assigned patents (including US 10,322,827 and US 10,144,532) do exist and do describe exotic electromagnetic field configurations claimed to produce large energy effects. Whether the devices work as described is contested: internal Navy reviews leaked to Drive magazine show skepticism among Navy technologists, and no working prototype has been publicly demonstrated.

claims.text: Free-energy and anti-gravity technology exists and is in active use by a shadow government of contractors operating outside US constitutional oversight.

evidence.quote:
these
contractors don't work for the US
government they work for the shadow
government

claims.rationale: The 'shadow government with its own navy and air force' framing is drawn from Steven Greer's Disclosure Project rhetoric. No whistleblower has produced verifiable artifacts (black-budget appropriation records, procurement receipts, hardware samples) supporting an off-books federal parallel structure of this scale. The circular reasoning - absence of evidence is proof of more powerful concealment - is a classic unfalsifiable-hypothesis pattern.

claims.text: Inventors of free-energy devices should publish their designs anonymously online rather than seek patents.

evidence.quote:
publish
your invention anonymously on the
internet and make it freely available

claims.rationale: The advice is internally inconsistent with the thesis: if suppression is all-powerful enough to kill patenters, an anonymous online publication that actually worked would be reproduced globally in days and could not be suppressed, falsifying the thesis. Conversely, if the designs don't work, the 'publish anonymously' move protects the inventor's reputation from the failure rather than from assassins.

dependencies.rationale: [logical] If anonymous online publication is a viable escape hatch, then a globe-spanning 100-year suppression is self-undermining.
